content: Hey Saturday, I need help getting our app relaunch budget finalized. We've got three engineering quotes on the table and I'm trying to figure out which vendor makes the most sense. The lead one is from TechWave at $32,000, but I also got quotes from DevShop at $28,500 and one from an individual contractor, Raj, at $30,000. What's your take on these—should I just go with the cheapest option?

Good question, and I'd suggest we don't make this purely about price. Let me break down what I'd recommend: TechWave's $32k is the highest, but if they have better availability and portfolio work in your space, that could be worth the extra cost. DevShop at $28.5k is compelling from a budget perspective, but I'd want to see their timeline—can they deliver in your sprint? Raj at $30k is interesting because individual contractors are often more flexible on scope changes, but there's risk if he gets overwhelmed or unavailable. Before you decide, what's your target timeline for the relaunch? That'll help us evaluate whether the cheaper option actually works for your schedule.

Timeline-wise, we need to launch by mid-August, so we're looking at about 10 weeks from now. That's pretty tight. TechWave said they could start immediately and have a full team, but DevShop mentioned they'd need 2 weeks to onboard another developer before hitting full capacity. Raj seems ready to go right away, which is good, but he's solo. I'm leaning toward either TechWave or Raj at this point given the timing constraint, but I'm also trying to be smart about total budget. What's your recommendation here?

Okay, so the 10-week timeline definitely eliminates that 2-week DevShop delay—you'd be giving up valuable time for an uncertain savings, which doesn't make sense. Between TechWave and Raj, here's how I'd think about it: TechWave's $32k gets you a team and redundancy (if someone gets sick, the project doesn't stall), plus professional project management overhead. Raj at $30k saves you $2k but you're betting on one person for a critical relaunch. Given the stakes, I'd actually lean toward Raj if he's genuinely experienced with mobile app architecture and you can lock in a completion date with penalties/bonuses. But here's the real question: what's your total budget ceiling for this whole relaunch? If we're working with a fixed number, that helps me advise whether the engineering spend should be $30k, $32k, or if we need to explore that $28.5k option despite the timeline risk.

That's what I was going to ask you about. Our investor gave us $47,500 total to work with for the full relaunch—both engineering and design. So if we're spending $30,000 on engineering with Raj, that leaves us $17,500 for design, which actually feels tight for a quality redesign. Does that seem reasonable to you, or should I be rethinking the engineering allocation?

Okay, so $47,500 total with $30k for engineering leaves $17.5k for design—let's think through this carefully. For a mobile app relaunch, design is honestly critical; it's what users see first. $17.5k could work if you're doing a focused redesign of core flows rather than a complete overhaul, but that's assuming you find a quality design agency that's hungry for the work and can be efficient. Here's what I'd suggest: let's get design quotes first to understand what quality costs at that $17.5k level. If the designers are saying "we'd need $22k to do this right," then we've got a problem and you'd need to either negotiate Raj down to $25k (unlikely), go with DevShop's $28.5k and cut design to $19k, or push back to investors for more budget. What design firms have you already approached, and do you have their estimates?

I haven't gotten design quotes yet, so that's my next step. But before I do that, I want to make sure we're locked in on the engineering side. I'm leaning toward Raj since he's faster and cheaper than TechWave, and the $30,000 figure works well for our $47,500 total budget. His quote reference is INV-2024-0391 if I need to track it. Should I just move forward with him, or is there anything else I should verify with Raj before signing?

Alright, so you're going with Raj at $30k (INV-2024-0391)—that locks in your engineering budget and leaves $17.5k for design, which is smart. Before you sign though, here are the critical things to verify with Raj: First, get a detailed statement of work that specifies exactly what's included in that $30k—does it cover testing, bug fixes, deployment, or is that extra? Second, what's his contingency if the scope creeps or you find major issues during development? Third, confirm he can commit full-time starting immediately and through mid-August with no conflicts. Fourth, ask about his backup plan if he gets sick or has an emergency—does he have a co-developer or partnership he can pull in, or are you entirely dependent on him? And finally, get it in writing that he won't take on conflicting projects during your 10-week window. Once you've got those answers and feel comfortable, you can sign off on INV-2024-0391 and we'll move to design quotes with your $17.5k ceiling.

Those are really good checks, thank you. I'm going to reach out to Raj today with those questions. In the meantime, for the design side, I'm thinking we should look for a smaller agency or a freelance lead designer rather than a big firm—maybe someone who's done SaaS product design before since that's closer to what we do. Do you have any suggestions on what to look for when I'm vetting designers, and should I be transparent upfront that our budget is $17.5k?

Great instinct going with a smaller agency or experienced freelancer—they tend to be more nimble and cost-effective than larger firms. For SaaS product design specifically, look for portfolio work that shows they understand user flows, onboarding optimization, and iterative design. When you reach out, I'd recommend being transparent about the $17.5k budget but frame it strategically: lead with the scope and timeline (10-week relaunch, mid-August deadline, focused on core flows) and then mention budget. That way they understand the constraints but aren't anchored to a number immediately. Ask candidates how they'd approach a design refresh within that budget—their answer tells you a lot about whether they're realistic and creative. Also ask if they include revisions in their estimate or if that's extra, because design almost always has a few rounds of feedback. When you get proposals, compare them not just on price but on what's actually included: are they doing user research, wireframes, high-fidelity mockups, design system documentation, handoff to engineering? That helps you understand if a lower quote is cheaper or just less comprehensive. Want me to help you draft a design brief to send out to candidates?

Yeah, I'd love help with the brief. But first, let me confirm the overall budget structure with you one more time just to make sure we're aligned: we've got $47,500 total, $30,000 going to Raj for engineering (that's INV-2024-0391), and $17,500 for design. I want to make sure I'm not missing anything or forgetting about other costs like hosting, marketing, or app store submission fees. Should those be separate line items in the budget, or are we treating this as purely the core product rebuild?

Perfect, let me confirm and clarify: yes, your structure is correct—$47,500 total budget, $30,000 to Raj for engineering (INV-2024-0391), and $17,500 for design. That $47,500 is the core product rebuild. However, you're right to think about those other line items, and they should absolutely be separate from this budget. Hosting costs, app store submission fees (both iOS and Android, ~$100 each), and any third-party tools or libraries that Raj needs should ideally come from a different operations budget or be discussed with your investors as add-on costs. Same with go-to-market and marketing—that's a separate conversation. The $47.5k is pure product: engineering, design, testing, and deployment. If Raj needs specific tools or services to complete the build, those should either be included in his $30k estimate or flagged as excluded so you can plan accordingly. I'd recommend adding that question to your conversation with Raj—ask him to clarify what his $30k quote covers and what external costs (if any) should be budgeted separately. Then we'll have a complete picture before you sign off on INV-2024-0391. Does that make sense?

That makes total sense. Okay, so I'm going to reach out to Raj today with those questions about the statement of work and what's included, and I'll also start drafting outreach to design firms. I'm thinking I'll aim for at least 3-4 design proposals so I can compare approaches and pricing within that $17,500 constraint. Once I have Raj's answers and at least 2-3 design quotes back, I want to set up a call with my co-founder to review everything and make the final vendor decisions. Should we plan that call for next week sometime?

Absolutely, that's a solid plan. Getting 3-4 design proposals gives you real comparison data and helps you see what $17.5k actually buys in the market—some might be more expensive, some cheaper, and you'll understand the tradeoffs. Next week makes sense for the co-founder call, especially since you need Raj's response first and a few days for design proposals to come back. I'd suggest aiming for mid-week (like Tuesday or Wednesday) to give yourself time through the weekend and early week to gather everything. By then you should have clarity on whether Raj can commit with full details on his $30k scope, and you'll have at least 2-3 solid design options to evaluate against your $17.5k budget. That positions you to actually make a decision on the call rather than just review incomplete information. One more thing: once you and your co-founder decide on vendors, have me help you draft approval language for your investor confirming that you're moving forward with the $47,500 spend broken down as $30k engineering, $17.5k design. That way everyone's on the same page about the final allocation. Sound good?

Perfect, that sounds like a great plan. I'm going to start moving on this today—reaching out to Raj, drafting the design brief, and getting those RFQs out to design firms. I'll come back to you by end of week with updates on what Raj says and which design proposals come back, and then we can prep for the co-founder call next week. Just to triple-check before I go: the budget is $47,500 total, right? $30,000 for engineering and $17,500 for design?

Exactly right—you've got it locked in. $47,500 total budget: $30,000 for engineering (Raj, INV-2024-0391), $17,500 for design. That's your constraint and your baseline for all vendor conversations going forward. When you're talking to design firms, if someone comes back at $20k, you know you either need to negotiate them down, cut scope, or have a conversation with your co-founder about reallocating from engineering. Similarly, if Raj's answers raise any concerns that you think he needs more budget, flag that early so you can make an informed decision. The sooner you get those RFQs out and hear back from Raj, the sooner you'll have a complete picture. Go ahead and move fast—you're on a 10-week timeline so every day counts. I'll be here when you're ready to review proposals or prep for that co-founder call. Good luck with the outreach!
